summary:
The post-mortem finding most consistent with this case is an elevated Reid index (>50%), indicating significant alveolar damage due to long-term cigarette smoke exposure leading to emphysema. This pattern fits well given his age, heavy tobacco use, chronic symptoms like cough and leg swelling from cor pulmonale secondary to COPD-related hypoxia.

Okay, I need to summarize Agent1’s reasoning based strictly on the requirements — only two sentences focusing solely on key clinical points related to the question.

First sentence should identify what the exam reveals and why it makes sense clinically using core facts: smoker, old age, weight gain, persistent cough, peripheral edema pointing toward right-heart strain → all point strongly towards COPD/emphysema as primary pathology.

Second sentence must explain how that manifests specifically at autopsy level via the Reid index being above threshold for severe emphysema caused by smoking.

I'll avoid any mention of other options unless necessary; keep focus tight on cause-effect chain linking lifestyle factors to histological findings observed after death.
